title: Quiz: Monte Carlo Option Pricing
description: Test your understanding of Monte Carlo simulation for option pricing

questions.question: How does Monte Carlo standard error scale with the number of simulations?
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo error scales as 1/√n, where n is the number of simulations. This means you need 4× more simulations to halve the error - a slow convergence rate that motivates variance reduction techniques.

questions.question: What is the key idea behind antithetic variates?

options.text: Use the same random numbers for all paths
Use Z and -Z together to reduce variance
Use a known analytical result as a benchmark
Increase the number of time steps

questions.explanation: Antithetic variates uses each random draw Z along with its negative -Z. If f(Z) is high when f(-Z) is low, averaging them reduces variance while maintaining the correct mean.

questions.question: What is an Asian option's payoff based on?

options.text: The average stock price over the option's life

questions.explanation: Asian options have payoffs based on the average price over the option's life, not just the final price. This averaging can be arithmetic or geometric, and makes the option less sensitive to price manipulation at expiry.

questions.question: In control variates, what is the 'control'?

options.text: A variable with known expectation correlated with the payoff

questions.explanation: The control variate is a variable whose expectation we know analytically. By measuring how far each simulation's control is from its known mean, we can adjust the payoff estimate to reduce variance.

questions.question: Monte Carlo can only price European options, not path-dependent options.
questions.explanation: False. Monte Carlo excels at pricing path-dependent options like Asian, barrier, and lookback options because it simulates full price paths. This is one of its main advantages over analytical methods.

questions.question: What happens to an up-and-out call if the stock price reaches the barrier?
questions.explanation: An up-and-out option is 'knocked out' (becomes worthless) if the underlying price rises above the barrier level at any point during the option's life.

questions.question: Why use the same random numbers when calculating Greeks via finite differences?

options.text: To reduce variance in the Greek estimate

questions.explanation: Using common random numbers (same seed) for both the bumped and base cases creates correlation between estimates, dramatically reducing the variance of the finite difference estimate.

questions.question: Which are valid variance reduction techniques? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Antithetic variates, control variates, and importance sampling are all valid variance reduction techniques. Using fewer simulations increases variance, not reduces it.
